video.title: Lego Batman: Legacy of the Dark Knight | Fully Ramblomatic

captions.text: Is there any contemporary IP that has been  adapted, reinterpreted, turned inside out,
and hung from a meat hook by its underpants as  much as Batman? Other than possibly the Bible—ooh,
keep your children away from my searing edge,  madame. Is the concept of a pointy-eared goth
twat with a nark on so compelling that it never  stops being interesting to ask, "What if the
pointy-eared goth twat was in the future?" "What  if the pointy-eared goth twat's archnemesis was a
Bakewell tart?" Or indeed, in the case of Lego  Batman, "What if the pointy-eared goth twat's
archnemesis was the underside of somebody's foot?"  But apparently Lego Batman is compelling enough an
idea that it wasn't sufficiently explored in  Lego Batman 1, 2 and 3, which is why we need
Lego Batman: Legacy of the Dark Knight. "Where  do we go with this one?" someone in the planning
stages must have asked. Then someone else  pointed out the sign that hangs permanently
in the Lego video games division: "If in doubt,  adapt another bloody film." "That's a point;
have we ever adapted any Batman movies?" Then an  insane person ran in and said, "Wait, have we ever
adapted EVERY BATMAN MOVIE SIMULTANEOUSLY?"  Surely that would be insane, insane person.
Whereupon Batman: Arkham Asylum popped its head  around the door going, "DID SOMEBODY SAY INSANE?"
In brief, Lego Batman: Legend of the Hidden Temple  assigns itself the daunting task of adapting
all of Batman in one go, including basically  every movie from the 1980s Batman onwards AND
the Arkhamverse games. Like, we start with an  adaptation of Batman Begins, which shifts into
Robert Pattinson's The Batman before we play  through the Joker's origin from Batman 1980,
but then instead of dying at the end, he turns  into Heath Ledger's Joker so we can adapt some
scenes from the Dark Knight a few chapters later.  But arbitrarily, we don't get to see Paul Dano's
Riddler dress up in a skin-tight green unitard,  so that image will have to stay relegated to my
dream journal. It's pretty amusing to watch the  cheerful neon plastic world of Lego tie itself in
knots trying to adapt some of the grittier moments  from the movies, such as—oh, nothing particularly
important, just THE MURDER OF BRUCE WAYNE'S  FUCKING PARENTS, in which the bodies just very
awkwardly don't appear on screen and we hastily  time jump a few years so we can watch Bruce Wayne
pratfalling on discarded fish some more. So what  do we end up with after madly smashing together
all these themes, tones, and interpretations?  Well, apparently, we end up with Alfred sounding
uncannily like David Bowie for some reason. And  more broadly, we have a complete fucking mess.
But this is one of many criticisms of Lego Batman:  Dark Style Legover in this video that's not going
to be particularly helpful. After all, mess can be  fun, especially for younger audiences. One could
argue that mess is the essence of Lego's appeal;  my kids certainly like making a mess with it on
the shagpile carpet, and it consequently makes a  mess of the soles of my feet. I might argue that
the game is so full of random crap it doesn't have  time to give any single element its due, important
plot elements are rushed through in flash  forwards, supervillains routinely defeated in
the same chapter they're introduced, the largest  segment of the plot revolving around the Joker,
obviously, but then he mysteriously disappears  so the finale can adapt The Dark Knight Rises
featuring a version of Bane introduced there and  then voiced with a slightly phoned-in Matt Berry
performance that just sounds like Matt Berry  doing his usual Matt Berry voice. But again,
this is missing the point; consistency of tone  and focused narrative are only things grown-ups
care about, and this is a Lego game for kiddies,  who judge the quality of their entertainment by
how ruined the living room couch is when they're  finished excitedly bouncing up and down on it.
This is also why it will be unhelpful of me to  complain that the game's too easy. 'Cause again,
kiddies. If it's challenge I'm after, I  should be holding out for Lego Sekiro.
The brief summary is that Lego Batman: Legally  Blonde is baby's first Arkham Knight. It takes
place across a sandbox map of Gotham City  that you navigate with a hookshot, glider,
and Batmobile that all feel familiar and yet  stripped down for the kiddies, like an Easy-Bake
Oven version. It's quite a few missions in before  we get to the sandbox map as we need time to
establish the origin story, and it wouldn't  have been much fun to navigate it as teenage
Bruce Wayne on a motor scooter. But perhaps there  we find my first non-pointless criticism, the one
thing you didn't borrow from the Arkham series and  probably should've: kicking the origin story in
the head and starting with Batman and his villains  already established, 'cause frankly, it's not that
interesting to watch Batman not being Batman.  And who the fuck doesn't know Batman's origin
story at this point? Even the kiddies know it. I'm  pretty sure they print it on the coloring pages at
Cheesecake Factory. But even when you get to the  sandbox map, there's not much point in engaging
with it and hunting for collectibles and Riddler  puzzles until post-ending fuckabouts 'cause most
of them require the abilities of characters  you unlock over the course of the story.
Most of which make sense—Catwoman can open safes,  Robin can win Mr. Lovely Legs competitions,
etc.—some of which not so much, like  Commissioner Gordon having a glue gun.
Maybe he just got called away from his home arts  and crafts project. Honestly though, post-story,
I struggled to find the enthusiasm to chip away at  the dense particle cloud of map icons because they
get repetitive very fast and I knew the only  rewards would be more suit and car cosmetics
to use while exploring the sandbox I now had no  reason to explore. When you're not in the sandbox
very emphatically not using the Batmobile to get  to places, you're in enclosed story missions,
all of which have their own collectibles and  challenges. Each mission has a group of five
hidden objects you're supposed to destroy,  which are occasionally hard to identify until
you get to the mission summary screen going,  "Duh! Why didn't you destroy the five rubber
ducks?" 'Cause they had alibis placing them  away from the scene of the crime? I dunno;
you never mentioned them. Combat is more or less,  again, the Saturday morning cartoon version of
Arkhamverse free-flow counter-based combat, and  I found it a bit boring and button-mashy and so
easy that I saw very little point in bothering  to engage with the stealth mechanics at all.
But again, we must consider this through the lens  of me being a grown-up whose brain does not run
exclusively on inadequately diluted Ribena. On the  whole, I'd say the game functions, and it's pretty
impressive how much they've crammed into it, but  I didn't like it much from a story and dialogue
standpoint. For one thing, I didn't find the  gags very funny. This isn't knee-jerk anti-Lego
prejudice—much as stepping on Lego certainly  makes my knees jerk—because believe it or not,
I've seen the Lego Batman movie, and I thought  it was pretty funny. This never gets to that
level. Feels like a large chunk of the gags are  based on referencing stuff and don't work if
you don't know the reference. One example that  jumps out in memory was in the boss fight with
Danny DeVito's Penguin where he says, "Anyway, I  started blasting" in reference to Danny DeVito's
character in Always Sunny in Philadelphia, but if  that goes over your head, it isn't funny at all,
and I know some of you are thinking it's funny  and may be more likely to buy the game now,
and I want you to understand that I  hate you for it. So gameplay's fine,
but even gags aside, the story doesn't really  go anywhere and creates a profound sense of,
"Haven't we been through all this before?"  Not unlike the Bay Area highway interchange.
